sound_type: harsh grating kraa-a-a -- the alpine broadcaster, far-carrying, mechanical, diagnostic for alpine habitat
ecosystem: Alpine pine -- whitebark pine (Pinus albicaulis) stands at and above treeline. Open, wind-swept alpine ridges.
season: Year-round resident. Caches seeds in late summer/fall; recovers through winter and spring. Seasonal altitudinal movements between caching and recovery sites.

coordinates.description: Reference for alpine pine habitat in the Cascades, Washington. Clark's Nutcrackers found in whitebark pine stands at and above treeline throughout the Cascade Range and Olympic Mountains.

taxonomy_note.description: SIXTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-105) -- the Corvidae Sextet. Nucifraga is a different genus from all previous corvids in the engine (Corvus, Cyanocitta, Perisoreus). NEW GENUS. Named for William Clark (Lewis and Clark expedition). Species name columbiana references the Columbia River region.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Corvidae count: 7 (Steller's Jay 53, Raven 100, Raven pair 101, American Crow 102, Steller's Jay raucous 103, Gray Jay 104, Clark's Nutcracker 105)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Nucifraga from Latin nux ('nut') + frangere ('to break') -- nut-breaker. columbiana = 'of the Columbia River region.' Named for William Clark, who first described the bird in his journal on August 22, 1805, near the Salmon River in present-day Idaho, during the Lewis and Clark expedition.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Nucifraga is new to the engine at degree 105. Three species worldwide: Clark's Nutcracker (N. columbiana, western North America), Spotted Nutcracker (N. caryocatactes, Eurasia), Kashmir Nutcracker (N. multipunctata, South Asia). All are pine seed specialists with extreme caching behavior.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Passeriformes. Sixth consecutive corvid passerine at degrees 100-105.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Corvidae continues its unprecedented run. Six consecutive degrees (100-105) ties the Mustelidae sextet (63-68) and the Sciuridae sextet (75-80) for the longest family streak since Sciuridae's octet (75-82).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,500-5,000 Hz. Harsh, grating, broadband call. The energy is distributed across a wide frequency range, contributing to the harsh quality.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 2,000-4,000 Hz. The grating quality comes from broadband energy with rough spectral characteristics.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary kraa-a-a: 2,000-4,500 Hz, harsh, nasal, grating. Chatter: broadband, rapid. Soft calls: narrower band, lower volume.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual kraa-a-a: 0.5-1.5 seconds. Often given in series. Chatter: 2-5 seconds. Vocalizations are prominent and frequent.
song_metrics.note_structure: HARSH GRATING KRAA-A-A: The primary vocalization is a drawn-out, nasal, grating call with rough spectral quality. Almost mechanical in character. No tonal sweetness. Far-carrying across open alpine terrain.
song_metrics.note_types: Kraa-a-a (primary, diagnostic), chatter (social, territorial), soft calls (close-range, pair communication). Limited mimicry compared to other corvids in the sextet.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Simple but emphatic. The kraa-a-a is often repeated in series -- kraa kraa kraa -- with spacing between calls. No complex phrase structure. The call is a broadcast, not a conversation.
song_metrics.timbre: Harsh. Grating. Nasal. Mechanical. Almost industrial in quality. Maximum contrast with the Gray Jay's soft whistles (104). The most abrasive timbre in the Corvidae sextet.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- high energy. Loud, carrying, persistent. The call echoes across alpine ridges and open terrain. Audible at hundreds of meters. The nutcracker is built for long-range communication in the open alpine landscape.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate to slow. Individual calls are drawn out (kraa-a-a). Series are spaced. The tempo reflects the open landscape -- calls need time to travel across alpine distances.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND SOCIAL. Given in flight (commonly), while perched, during foraging, in territorial disputes, and as alarm. The kraa-a-a is the primary all-purpose vocalization. Less vocal diversity than the raven; more vocal than the crow.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Moderate. Some geographic variation in call structure documented. Individual variation present but the harsh grating quality is consistent across range.
song_metrics.key_property: HARSH GRATING KRAA-A-A -- the alpine broadcaster. E=5. Harsh, nasal, mechanical, far-carrying. Whitebark pine obligate -- caches 98,000 seeds per year. Sublingual pouch carries 150 seeds. Greatest spatial memory in the corvid family. Named for William Clark (Lewis and Clark expedition). SIXTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-105). The Corvidae Sextet. Forty-fifth consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Medium-large corvid. Pale gray body. Black wings with prominent white wing patches (secondary coverts). Black central tail feathers with white outer tail feathers. Long, sharp, slightly decurved black bill. Sturdy build.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 105: (1) PALE GRAY BODY -- lighter than any other corvid in the sextet. (2) BLACK WINGS with LARGE WHITE PATCHES -- highly visible in flight. (3) LONG SHARP BILL -- a seed-extraction tool, noticeably longer and more pointed than other corvids. (4) ALPINE HABITAT -- if you see a gray bird with black-and-white wings above treeline, it is a Clark's Nutcracker.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Juveniles similar to adults but slightly duller.
plumage.flight_pattern: Direct, strong flight. White wing patches and white outer tail feathers create a distinctive black-and-white flash pattern in flight. Often seen flying from ridgeline to ridgeline with the harsh kraa-a-a call.

physical_description.overall: Medium-large corvid. Body length 27-30 cm. Wingspan 55-61 cm. Weight 106-161 g. Pale gray body, black wings with white patches, long sharp bill. Sturdy build adapted for seed extraction and high-elevation living.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 105: pale gray bird with black-and-white wings and a long sharp bill in alpine pine habitat. The bill is the key -- longer and more pointed than any other corvid's. The white wing patches flash in flight.
physical_description.field_id: Pale gray corvid in alpine or high-elevation pine forest. Black wings with large white patches. Long, sharp, slightly curved bill. Harsh grating kraa-a-a call. If you are above treeline in the Cascades and see a gray bird with flashing white wing patches calling harshly -- it is a Clark's Nutcracker.

nodes.title: Harsh Grating Kraa-a-a -- The Alpine Broadcaster
nodes.description: Harsh, grating, far-carrying call. E=5. Diagnostic for alpine habitat. The most abrasive timbre in the Corvidae sextet. Mechanical quality. Echoes across alpine ridges. The loudest voice at the highest elevation.

nodes.title: Sixth Consecutive Corvidae (100-105) -- The Corvidae Sextet
nodes.description: Raven (100, deep cronk), Raven pair (101, duet), American Crow (102, sharp caw), Steller's Jay (103, raucous alarm), Gray Jay (104, soft whistles), Clark's Nutcracker (105, harsh grating). Six degrees, one family. Ties Mustelidae sextet (63-68) and Sciuridae sextet (75-80).

nodes.title: Whitebark Pine Mutualism -- Obligate Seed Disperser
nodes.description: 98,000 seeds per year. Sublingual pouch carries 150 seeds. Unretrieved seeds germinate. Without the nutcracker, the pine cannot reproduce. Obligate mutualism. Whitebark pine in crisis (blister rust, beetles, climate change).

nodes.title: Seed Caching -- Greatest Spatial Memory in the Corvid Family
nodes.description: 98,000 caches per year. Enlarged hippocampus. Recovers caches under snow using spatial landmarks. Transport distance up to 22 km. 70-90% recovery rate. Unrecovered seeds are the foundation of alpine forest regeneration.

nodes.title: Alpine Pine Habitat -- The Highest Corvid Elevation
nodes.description: Whitebark pine stands at and above treeline. Above the Gray Jay's subalpine forest (104). Shared alpine with Pika (74), Hoary Marmot (76). Named for William Clark (Lewis and Clark expedition, 1805). The corvid of the highest elevations.

cross_references.relationship: CORVIDAE SEXTET (100-105). Gray Jay in subalpine conifer (E=4 soft whistles); nutcracker in alpine pine (E=5 harsh grating). Adjacent corvids occupying adjacent elevational bands. Maximum acoustic contrast: softest voice (104) followed by harshest voice (105).

CORVIDAE SEXTET (100-105). Intelligence. Both demonstrate extraordinary spatial memory and food caching. The raven is generalist; the nutcracker is the most specialized cacher in the corvid family (98,000 seeds/year).

ALPINE NEIGHBOR. Both occupy alpine habitat. Both cache food for winter -- the pika in hay piles on talus, the nutcracker in soil across the alpine landscape. Parallel survival strategies at the highest elevations.
ALPINE SHARED HABITAT. Both are signature species of the alpine zone. The marmot hibernates; the nutcracker caches and endures. Different winter survival strategies in the same environment.
SUBALPINE/ALPINE OVERLAP. Marten in dense subalpine forest; nutcracker in open alpine pine stands. Both year-round high-elevation residents. Different structural habitat requirements.
